revision.text:
{{神社| 名称=名木林神社| 所在地=岐阜県安八郡安八町大森1479-1| 祭神=[[天照大神]] [[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]| 社格=郷社| 例祭=| 創建=不明| }}
'''名木林神社'''（なぎばやしじんじゃ）は、[[岐阜県]][[安八郡]][[安八町]]にある[[神社]]。
[[元伊勢]]の[[美濃国]]伊久良河宮の論社の一つであり、[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は郷社。別称は神明宮、藤の宮<ref>旧所在地の境内に[[藤]]の老木があったことから。</ref>など。
一説では鎮座する安八町[[大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|大森]]<ref>http://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=42&shrname=%E2%98%85%E5%90%8D%E6%9C%A8%E6%9E%97%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E2%98%85 名木林神社詳細 - 岐阜県神社庁</ref>の地名の由来となった神社であり、後の[[名森村]]の村名の由来<ref>「木」「林」を合わせて「森」とした。</ref>となった神社でもある。
== 沿革・概略 == * 創建年代は不明。[[平安時代]]の美濃国神明帳では「正五位名木林明神」と記載されている。「名木林」は、かつては「なぎばやし」では無く「なぎはや」と呼称されていたという。 * [[1872年]]（明治5年）、郷社となる。 * [[1934年]]（昭和9年）8月30日、[[木曽川]]上流改修工事により境内は新たな河道となるため、旧所在地（当時の安八郡名森村大字大森字宮東）から現在地に移転する。
== 祭神 == * [[天照大神]] * [[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]
== 参考文献 == * 安八町誌
== 脚注 == <references />
{{DEFAULTSORT:なきはやししんしや}} [[Category:岐阜県の神社]] [[Category:岐阜県の旧郷社]] [[Category:安八町]] [[Category:安八町の歴史]]

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA232 |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |date=2010-12-28 |publisher=Scarecrow Press |isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Dougill |first=John |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-H_cEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT204 |title=Off the Beaten Tracks in Japan: A Journey by Train from Hokkaido to Kyushu |date=2023-11-07 |publisher=Stone Bridge Press, Inc. |isbn=978-1-61172-963-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Army |first=United States Department of the |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PvgsAAAAIAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=RA3-PA147 |title=Pamphlet - Dept. of the Army |date=1945 |publisher=Headquarters, Department of the Army. |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MVHoAAAAMAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA147 |title=Department of the Army Pamphlet |date=1945 |publisher=The Department |language=en}}</ref>

==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|qq=|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|qq=|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|qq=|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

== Enshrined deities ==

[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu]]

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|qq=|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|qq=|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|qq=|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|qq=|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|qq=|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|qq=|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|qq=|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|qq=|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|qq=|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

{{afc comment|1=content originally from [[Draft:Moto-Ise Shrines]] see that page history for attribution[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 06:22, 3 December 2023 (UTC)}}

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA232 |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |date=2010-12-28 |publisher=Scarecrow Press |isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Dougill |first=John |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-H_cEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT204 |title=Off the Beaten Tracks in Japan: A Journey by Train from Hokkaido to Kyushu |date=2023-11-07 |publisher=Stone Bridge Press, Inc. |isbn=978-1-61172-963-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Army |first=United States Department of the |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PvgsAAAAIAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=RA3-PA147 |title=Pamphlet - Dept. of the Army |date=1945 |publisher=Headquarters, Department of the Army. |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MVHoAAAAMAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA147 |title=Department of the Army Pamphlet |date=1945 |publisher=The Department |language=en}}</ref>

==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building
| name             = Nagibayashi Shrine
| location         = 1479-1 Ōmori, Anpachi Town, Anpachi District, Gifu Prefecture, Japan

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

{{afc comment|1=content originally from [[Draft:Moto-Ise Shrines]] see that page history for attribution[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 06:22, 3 December 2023 (UTC)}}

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA232 |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |date=2010-12-28 |publisher=Scarecrow Press |isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Dougill |first=John |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-H_cEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT204 |title=Off the Beaten Tracks in Japan: A Journey by Train from Hokkaido to Kyushu |date=2023-11-07 |publisher=Stone Bridge Press, Inc. |isbn=978-1-61172-963-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Army |first=United States Department of the |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PvgsAAAAIAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=RA3-PA147 |title=Pamphlet - Dept. of the Army |date=1945 |publisher=Headquarters, Department of the Army. |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MVHoAAAAMAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA147 |title=Department of the Army Pamphlet |date=1945 |publisher=The Department |language=en}}</ref>

==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building
| name             = Nagibayashi Shrine
| location         = 1479-1 Ōmori, Anpachi Town, Anpachi District, Gifu Prefecture, Japan

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA232 |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |date=2010-12-28 |publisher=Scarecrow Press |isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Dougill |first=John |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-H_cEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT204 |title=Off the Beaten Tracks in Japan: A Journey by Train from Hokkaido to Kyushu |date=2023-11-07 |publisher=Stone Bridge Press, Inc. |isbn=978-1-61172-963-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. ==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building

'''Nagibayashi Shrine''' (名木林神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Anpachi District, Gifu|Anpachi District]], [[:en:Anpachi, Gifu|Anpachi]].

It is one of the claimants to the title of the Iku-ragawamiya of {{ill|Moto-Ise|de|Moto-Ise|ja|元伊勢|zh|元伊势|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise|1=Moto Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387775}} in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], and its former shrine rank was Gōsha. Alternative names include Shinmei Shrine and Fuji ShrineNamed for an ancient {{ill|wisteria|de|Glyzinie|ja|藤|zh|紫藤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wisteria|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to フジ (植物)}} in the former precincts..

According to one theory, this shrine gave its name to the local area of {{ill|Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|de|Dorf Ōmori (Kreis Anpachi, Gifu)|ja|大森村 (岐阜県安八郡)|zh|大森村 (岐阜县安八郡)|lt=Ōmori|lt_ja=大森|lt_de=Ōmori|lt_zh=大森|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmori Village (Anpachi District, Gifu)|1=Ōmori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21652643}}, and later to {{ill|Namori Village|de|Namori-Dorf|ja|名森村|zh|名森村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namori Village|1=Namori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11415479}}.The characters for "tree" and "forest" were combined to form "forest" (森).

== History and Overview ==

The founding date is unknown. In the Heian period's shrine register of Mino Province it is listed as "Shōgoi Nagibayashi Myōjin." The name "Nagibayashi" was once pronounced "Nagihaya."

In [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), it was designated as a Gōsha.

On [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) August 30, due to riverbed changes from construction on the upper [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], the shrine was moved from its former location (then Ōmori, Namori Village, Anpachi District) to its present site.

{{afc comment|1=content originally from [[Draft:Moto-Ise Shrines]] see that page history for attribution[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 06:22, 3 December 2023 (UTC)}}

[[Nakibayashi Shrine]] is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}}

{{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref> The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]).<ref name="greenshinto">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en}}</ref> Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].<ref name=":0" /> 

More sources to add<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA232 |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |date=2010-12-28 |publisher=Scarecrow Press |isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Dougill |first=John |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-H_cEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT204 |title=Off the Beaten Tracks in Japan: A Journey by Train from Hokkaido to Kyushu |date=2023-11-07 |publisher=Stone Bridge Press, Inc. |isbn=978-1-61172-963-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Army |first=United States Department of the |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PvgsAAAAIAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=RA3-PA147 |title=Pamphlet - Dept. of the Army |date=1945 |publisher=Headquarters, Department of the Army. |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MVHoAAAAMAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA147 |title=Department of the Army Pamphlet |date=1945 |publisher=The Department |language=en}}</ref>

==Overview==

{{Infobox religious building
| name             = Nagibayashi Shrine
